params.text:
Diabetic emergencies

People with diabetes can have blood sugar that becomes too low or too high.

  • Hypoglycaemia (low blood sugar) comes on quickly. Signs include pale, sweaty skin, trembling, confusion, irritability and weakness. If the person is conscious and can swallow, give a sugary drink or snack such as fruit juice, a sugary (non-diet) fizzy drink or glucose tablets.
  • Hyperglycaemia (high blood sugar) comes on gradually. Signs include excessive thirst, needing to pass urine often, warm dry skin and breath that smells fruity or of pear drops.

Call 999 if the person is unresponsive, has a seizure, or does not improve within about 10 minutes of taking sugar. If in doubt, giving sugar to a conscious person is the safer first step.

The recovery position

If a casualty is unresponsive but breathing normally, place them in the recovery position. Lying on their side with the head tilted back keeps the airway open and lets fluids drain from the mouth, reducing the risk of choking. Once they are on their side, tilt the head back, check they are still breathing, and monitor them closely until the ambulance arrives. Be ready to turn them onto their back and start CPR if breathing stops.

params.question: A resident with diabetes becomes pale, shaky, sweaty and confused, but is awake and able to swallow. What is the best first response?

answers.text: Give them a sugary drink or snack, such as fruit juice or glucose tablets

tipsAndFeedback.chosenFeedback: Correct. These are signs of a hypo (low blood sugar). Fast-acting sugar for a conscious person is the right first step.

answers.text: Encourage them to lie down and rest without eating or drinking

tipsAndFeedback.chosenFeedback: No. Resting alone will not raise low blood sugar. Give fast-acting sugar while they are still able to swallow.

answers.text: Give them their insulin to bring things back to normal

tipsAndFeedback.chosenFeedback: No. Insulin lowers blood sugar further and could be dangerous. These signs suggest low sugar — give sugar, not insulin.

answers.text: Wait to see if the symptoms pass on their own

tipsAndFeedback.chosenFeedback: No. A hypo can worsen quickly. Act now by giving a sugary drink or snack, and call 999 if they do not improve.
